title: Johnson & Johnson — Neuroscience — briefing Q3-2025
html:
J&J Neuroscience Quarterly Briefing — Q3 2025

Quarter: July–September 2025 Earnings reported: J&J October 14 | Axsome October 2025 | Compass Pathways — pre-commercial Generated: 2026-04-27

Governing Thought

SPRAVATO grew 60.8% in Q3 — the strongest growth yet — driven by certified center expansion and MDD-SI uptake. But the competitive picture is evolving faster than SPRAVATO's growth: Auvelity crossed ~$250M annually with 80%+ growth, generic ketamine clinics exceeded 3,000, and COMP360's Phase 3 COMP006 is enrolling rapidly. Q3 is the quarter where SPRAVATO's demand story is strongest — and where the competitive threats are first becoming visible enough to warrant tracking.

1. J&J Financial Signals

Metric Q3 2025 Commentary
SPRAVATO growth +60.8% YoY Strongest quarterly growth to date; demand not saturating
J&J total $24.0B 6.8% growth; SPRAVATO a named growth driver
SPRAVATO quarterly ~$380M est. Approaching $1.5B annual run rate
  • Sequential acceleration confirms demand pull is strengthening
  • SPRAVATO growth exceeds all J&J branded drugs except DARZALEX and TREMFYA
  • Neuroscience becoming a credible third growth pillar alongside oncology and immunology

What J&J management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "Strongest growth quarter" for SPRAVATO — management explicitly flagged Q3 as the peak growth quarter to date, using superlative language that signals internal confidence in the trajectory. SPRAVATO was named alongside DARZALEX, TREMFYA, and ERLEADA as a company growth pillar.
  2. Certified center expansion highlighted as the growth driver — management attributed the 60.8% growth to increasing the number of certified treatment centers. The infrastructure investment is paying off — more centers = more patient access = more scripts.
  3. J&J framed Q3 as "proof of portfolio diversification beyond COVID" — SPRAVATO's neuroscience contribution was positioned in the context of J&J's strategic narrative about diversified growth. The company is explicitly using SPRAVATO to demonstrate it's not a single-franchise story.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts didn't push on competition — in Q3, no analyst raised Auvelity, generic ketamine, or COMP360 as competitive concerns for SPRAVATO. This is significant because it suggests the Street views SPRAVATO as having a clear competitive moat — a perception that may change as competitors mature.
  2. No questions about growth sustainability — analysts accepted 60.8% growth without challenging the duration. The absence of skepticism may reflect confidence in the TRD market's underpenetration, or it may reflect insufficient attention to competitive dynamics.
  3. The REMS model was not questioned — analysts didn't ask whether the in-clinic monitoring requirement limits SPRAVATO's addressable market. Given that 3,000+ generic ketamine clinics bypass REMS entirely, this is a gap in the analyst discourse.

2. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Growth Key Signal
Axsome Auvelity ~$70M +85% MDD augmentation capturing patients before SPRAVATO referral
Compass COMP360 Pre-revenue N/A COMP005 Phase 3 positive (2024); COMP006 enrolling; NDA planning
BMS Cobenfy ~$20M Launch Schizophrenia new mechanism; same prescriber base
SPRAVATO ~$380M +60.8% 5x Auvelity but Auvelity growing faster

Axsome Therapeutics — Auvelity (~$70M Q3, ~$250M annual run rate)

Axsome Q3 2025 (October 2025):

  1. Auvelity finding the MDD augmentation sweet spot: At ~$70M quarterly, Auvelity is establishing itself as the go-to novel oral mechanism for MDD patients who've failed 1-2 antidepressants. Psychiatrists describe it as "the step between SSRI failure and SPRAVATO referral."

  2. Axsome increasing promotional investment: With a dedicated sales force in psychiatry, Axsome is building mindshare with the same psychiatrists who prescribe SPRAVATO. SOV in psychiatric offices is rising, though still well below J&J/AbbVie levels.

  3. No TRD-specific data yet: Auvelity's FDA label is for MDD augmentation, not TRD specifically. But off-label use in mild/moderate TRD is growing. Axsome has discussed potential TRD studies.

Implication for J&J: Auvelity at ~$250M annual run rate is a leading indicator. The "step before SPRAVATO" positioning means the MDD-to-TRD referral funnel is being intercepted. If 10-15% of patients who would have been referred to SPRAVATO respond to Auvelity instead, that's ~$150-200M in reduced addressable market over time.

Compass Pathways — COMP360 (Phase 3 enrolling)

Compass Pathways Q3 2025 (clinical updates):

  1. COMP005 Phase 3 met primary endpoint (2024): The first positive Phase 3 for psilocybin in TRD established proof-of-concept. COMP006 is the confirmatory trial — currently enrolling, with completion expected Q4 2025.

  2. Conference presentations building physician awareness: Academic psychiatrists are discussing psilocybin-assisted therapy at major conferences (APA, ECNP). The narrative is shifting from "experimental" to "potentially practice-changing" — even before COMP006 results.

  3. Timeline: COMP006 readout Q1 2026 → NDA 2026 → potential approval 2027: This 18-month timeline means J&J has time to prepare but the clock is visible.

Implication for J&J: COMP360 is not yet a revenue competitor but is an attention competitor. The psilocybin conversation is consuming psychiatrist mindshare. J&J needs to position SPRAVATO's track record (5+ years of real-world data, insurance coverage, REMS infrastructure) as advantages before COMP360 reframes the narrative.

Generic Ketamine Ecosystem

  1. 3,000+ clinics by Q3 2025: Accelerating growth from 2,500+ in Q2. Telehealth-enabled at-home ketamine is the fastest-growing segment.

  2. Market size ~$500-600M annualized: A meaningful revenue pool that exists outside SPRAVATO's ecosystem.

  3. Patient overlap is real: ZoomRx tracking data suggests 25-30% of ketamine clinic patients would meet SPRAVATO clinical criteria if they had access to certified centers.

Implication for J&J: The generic ketamine market is SPRAVATO's largest competitive threat by revenue. J&J can't compete on price ($200-500 per ketamine session vs. $700-900 per SPRAVATO session), but can compete on evidence, standardization, and insurance coverage.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. SPRAVATO is growing in a market that's fragmenting: The TRD/depression space now has: (a) traditional antidepressants (generic, declining), (b) Auvelity (oral novel mechanism, growing fast), (c) SPRAVATO (intranasal REMS, growing fast), (d) generic ketamine (3,000+ clinics, price advantage), (e) TMS (gaining insurance coverage), and (f) COMP360 (on the horizon). SPRAVATO must hold its position as the "gold standard for TRD" amid increasing options.

  2. The competitive threats are at different stages but converging: Auvelity is NOW, generic ketamine is NOW, COMP360 is 18 months away. The combined effect will be felt in 2026-2027.

  3. SPRAVATO's REMS infrastructure is simultaneously a moat and a ceiling: 3,000+ certified centers = physician familiarity and insurance coverage. But the same REMS limits access in areas without certified centers, pushing patients to generic ketamine or Auvelity.

3. Clinical/Regulatory

  • SPRAVATO: Real-world evidence accumulating; MDD-SI uptake growing
  • COMP005 (Compass): Phase 3 met primary endpoint (2024); COMP006 enrolling
  • CAPLYTA (J&J acquisition): Adjunctive MDD — integrating into neuroscience portfolio
  • Aticaprant (J&J): Phase 3 for MDD — potential oral complement to SPRAVATO
  • MDMA (Lykos): FDA rejected MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD (2024) — creates psychedelic class uncertainty
  • Accelerated TMS: Stanford SAINT protocol gaining commercial traction (5-day intensive)

4. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Evidence This Quarter
1. SPRAVATO growth has significant runway CONFIRMED 60.8% growth is the peak so far; demand not saturating
2. Auvelity is the upstream interceptor EMERGING ~$250M annual run rate; "step before SPRAVATO" positioning gaining traction
3. COMP360 is the existential threat ON THE HORIZON COMP005 positive; COMP006 enrolling; 18-month timeline to potential approval
4. Generic ketamine is the "good enough" threat CONFIRMED 3,000+ clinics; $500-600M market; 25-30% patient overlap with SPRAVATO criteria
5. J&J's portfolio defense BUILDING CAPLYTA acquisition complete; aticaprant Phase 3 advancing

5. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q4 2025)

  1. SPRAVATO Q4 growth — can it sustain >60%, or does the competitive landscape start biting?
  2. COMP006 enrollment completion — full enrollment expected Q4; readout early 2026
  3. Auvelity quarterly revenue — crossing $80M+ quarterly signals acceleration
  4. Generic ketamine FDA actions — any regulatory action on compounded ketamine
  5. Aticaprant Phase 3 updates — J&J's oral pipeline defense

6. Conversation Starters for J&J

  1. "Auvelity grew 85% in Q3 to ~$70M quarterly. Axsome is explicitly positioning it as the 'step before SPRAVATO.' That means your referral funnel is being intercepted. We should measure whether psychiatrists are trying Auvelity before referring to certified centers — and if so, how many patients are 'captured' and never reach SPRAVATO." — Competitor data driving upstream research design.

  2. "Generic ketamine clinics are now 3,000+ and growing. They capture ~$500-600M annually in revenue that could theoretically go to SPRAVATO. Your REMS is the quality differentiator — but are psychiatrists and patients AWARE of the quality difference? We should be measuring SPRAVATO vs. generic ketamine perception." — Market dynamics driving tracking opportunity.

  3. "COMP006 readout is 6 months away. If it's positive — and COMP005 was — the psilocybin NDA is filed in 2026. The pre-competitive intelligence window is NOW. We should be measuring psychiatrist awareness, interest, and perceived positioning of COMP360 vs. SPRAVATO before the data shapes the narrative." — Forward-looking competitive intelligence BD.

Sources: J&J Q3 2025 (Oct 14), Axsome Q3 2025 (Oct 2025), Compass Pathways clinical updates (Q3 2025).

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
